routed_to_questions.q_text: In supply / procurement / S&OP — across the FMCG companies you watch — how much AI activity is happening today versus 12 months ago?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: P&G actively building LLM models + bots so sales reps can query supply availability and production schedules during customer negotiation. Vision stated; not yet in production. Stack Box India named as the live supply-network vendor P&G uses extensively (incl. Indonesia ops).
routed_to_questions.evidence: Using developing bots, developing LLM models to create certain things. For example, currently, if the salesperson has to go and negotiate with the customer, he calls up: 'Oh, there's a big incoming, can we get the supply?' Party needs to query — can it be made? An assistant can get all the data for you, RPM, errors, past production schedule. Not yet there. But that's where the vision is. ... Stack Box India is a leading company in supplier network optimization. P&G uses them extensively, including Indonesia operations.

routed_to_questions.q_text: For plant-floor AI — predictive maintenance, OEE, ML-vision QC, batch-process optimisation — what's actually deployed in production today?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: At P&G, plant-floor 'process engineering' AI is not new — they've used optimization/simulation for the sequencing problem for 10 years. The 'new AI' wave is NOT showing up on the plant floor; it's manifesting in decision-support (RGM, demand) instead. Refutes the thesis claim that plant-floor AI is an active opportunity at tier-1 incumbents.
routed_to_questions.evidence: Process engineering — no. If you are in a more current it's like production sequencing, and production planning... maybe there's no production planning, either you add it to supply and procurement. So production planning can be optimized. We have used / been using for 10 years the sequencing problem, but not with the eye, but a little bit of simulation tool demand.

routed_to_questions.q_text: For the demand side in FMCG — modern trade / general trade / quick commerce / D2C — what's the most-used AI capability today?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Manu's strongest signal of the call. Trade promotion / RGM (pricing + promo + channel-impact analysis) is the single largest leadership-time-sink at P&G and the most automatable demand-side area. Marketing leadership + digital marketing explicitly tagged 'definitely' as AI nodes; key accounts/channels framed as 'a big part of trade'.
routed_to_questions.evidence: Digital marketing, definitely. Marketing leadership, definitely. ... One big part of trade and accounts and channels — planning a promotion. Pricing and promotion decision. That takes a lot of time of the leadership level. What pricing, where, what is the impact of this pricing on other channel? What does my price tier do? It's super critical. But that's another area can be automated when looking at data.

routed_to_questions.q_text: When you think about S&OP / Demand Planning specifically — what 2-3 things are eating the most leadership / FTE time?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Manu pinpointed domain expertise as the single biggest barrier to demand-planning AI at P&G: 'people think demand planning is statistical' but the real factors are off-take, distributor behaviour, false-signal promos (10+1 schemes). This is what's eating leadership/FTE time — not data, but reconciling demand drivers.
routed_to_questions.evidence: The biggest barrier is the domain expertise. They think demand planning is in terms of numbers — a mathematical model, statistical model, looking at data. But if I say demand planning, what are the demand planning factors? Number one, there is a demand signal. There's off-take which we are getting [from distributors]. 10+1 promotions create false demand signals.

routed_to_questions.q_text: For S&OP / Demand Planning — what AI / automation is actually being used today?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: P&G is *trying* — building bots/LLM models — but explicit 'not yet there'. Statistical/mathematical models existed pre-AI. The gap between statistical demand planning (decade-old) and true AI demand planning (in build, not shipped) is what makes this a mixed signal.
routed_to_questions.evidence: We're trying. We've been trying. Using developing bots, developing LLM models. But not yet there — that's where the vision is. Half [the systems are] in Excel, half undocumented. Need to map existing systems before AI implementation.

routed_to_questions.q_text: If a credible vendor showed up tomorrow with $500K committed budget for S&OP / Demand Planning, what would you spend it on first?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Manu's wishlist is a sales-rep AI assistant that queries supply/production data during live negotiation — concrete use case named as the next investment vector. Domain-knowledge mapping is the precondition before any tool buy.
routed_to_questions.evidence: If the salesperson has to go and negotiate with the customer, he calls up: 'Can we get the supply?' Party needs to query — can it be made? An assistant can get all the data for you. RPM, errors, past production schedule. Not yet there. But that's where the vision is.

routed_to_questions.q_text: For Supply Planning — what AI / automation is actually being used today?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Manu named Stack Box India as the live supply-network vendor in production at P&G (specifically called out Indonesia ops). Concrete vendor-attribution. P&G Indonesia claim not yet independently corroborated — testimony-grade signal, treat as confirmation by P&G insider.
routed_to_questions.evidence: Stack Box India is a leading company in supplier network optimization. P&G uses them extensively. Indonesia operations especially.

routed_to_questions.q_text: When you think about Process Engineering specifically — what 2-3 things are eating the most leadership / FTE time?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Manu walked through the FMCG node list and said process engineering is 'NO' — meaning not an active AI opportunity at tier-1 FMCG. routed_to_questions.evidence: Process engineering — no. It's production sequencing and production planning... we have been using for 10 years the sequencing problem, but not with the eye, but a little bit of simulation. Tool demand.

routed_to_questions.q_text: For Process Engineering — what AI / automation is actually being used today?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Same as Q24 — 10 years of simulation, no AI. P&G is the highest-AI-maturity FMCG in India and they don't see process engineering as the next AI frontier.
routed_to_questions.evidence: We have been using for 10 years the sequencing problem, but not with the eye, but a little bit of simulation. Tool demand.

routed_to_questions.q_text: For Marketing Leadership — what AI / automation is actually being used today?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Manu marked marketing leadership and digital marketing as 'definitely' AI-active at P&G. Concrete vendor engagement: P&G leadership visited TCS T-HUB; also engaging external AI partners directly. Adoption mode: PoC-gated.
routed_to_questions.evidence: Digital marketing, definitely. Marketing leadership, definitely. ... It's happening both ways. We went to TCS HUB — our leadership team was there. But we are also engaging. We are learning. We are eager. So both ways it's happening.

routed_to_questions.q_text: When you think about Trade & Digital Marketing specifically — what 2-3 things are eating the most leadership / FTE time?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Strongest Manu signal of the call. RGM (pricing + promotion + channel-impact analysis) named as the single largest leadership-time-sink at P&G and the most-automatable decision-AI node. Validates the thesis's emphasis on RGM/trade-marketing AI.
routed_to_questions.evidence: One big part of trade and accounts and channels — planning a promotion. Pricing and promotion decision. That takes a lot of time of the leadership level. What pricing, where, what is the impact of this pricing on other channel? What does my price tier do? It's super critical. But that's another area can be automated when looking at data.

routed_to_questions.q_text: For Trade & Digital Marketing — what AI / automation is actually being used today?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Active build at P&G — bots + LLM models for trade/digital marketing. Engaging external AI vendors + TCS T-HUB exploration. Adoption is PoC-gated.
routed_to_questions.evidence: We're trying. Using developing bots, developing LLM models. ... We went to TCS HUB. Our leadership team was there. But we are also engaging. We are learning. We are eager.

routed_to_questions.q_text: When you think about Sales Leadership specifically — what 2-3 things are eating the most leadership / FTE time?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Manu carved sales leadership out of pure decision-automation: 'sales is relationship building' — AI augments but doesn't replace decision-making. Reframes the thesis claim narrowly. Needs a tiebreaker per directive §3.
routed_to_questions.evidence: Sales leadership — I have my doubt, because sales is... it can augment it, but it has more relationship building.

routed_to_questions.q_text: For Sales Leadership — what AI / automation is actually being used today?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Sales-rep AI assistant in build at P&G — LLM-based query tool so reps can pull supply/production data live during customer negotiation. Concrete use case, in development, not yet shipped.
routed_to_questions.evidence: If the salesperson has to go and negotiate with the customer, he calls up: 'Oh there's a big incoming, can we get the supply?' Party needs to query — can it be made? An assistant can get all the data for you, RPM, errors, past production schedule. Not yet there. But that's where the vision is.

routed_to_questions.q_text: When you think about Key Accounts & Channels specifically — what 2-3 things are eating the most leadership / FTE time?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Tightly coupled with the trade-promo signal. Manu sees pricing-decision + cross-channel impact analysis as the leadership-time-sink; channel planning + promo design are the levers.
routed_to_questions.evidence: Sales leadership, what you call — you can call it then PNP promotion and planning so much. One big part of trade — accounts and channels. Planning a promotion. Pricing and promotion decision. That takes a lot of time of the leadership level.

routed_to_questions.q_text: Look at the work your team did last week — across the workflow, where is AI today addressing real bottlenecks vs. where would you go next?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Manu independently validated the thesis B2C/FMCG vs B2B/auto-ancillary cut: variability of demand makes FMCG decision-AI the bigger value pool; auto-ancillary is stable demand, so the value is in physical automation not decision optimization. Direct restatement of the thesis weighting.
routed_to_questions.evidence: If you are in a B2C space, which is more of FMCG and also auto-ancillary supplying B2B — you are supplying to demand, and there's variability there. That makes it easy to disrupt. ... Currently all the automated lines on a car line — you don't shut them and start them, you run at particular speed. The ancillary company is supplying to an auto company; they're truly dependent on a very, very predictable demand. ... In the FMCG sector B2C, it'll be more efficient decisions than humans because of [variability] — that's the difference between B2B and B2C.

routed_to_questions.q_text: Of the 5 FMCG peers you watch most closely, who's furthest ahead on actually deploying AI in operations?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: P&G is on the leading curve among FMCG incumbents — Manu's frame is that the threat isn't peers but upstarts. Tier-1 FMCG mainly competes via change-management speed, not algorithm sophistication. Carve-out: Mamaearth-shape DTC asset-light brands are OUT of the manufacturing-AI thesis.
routed_to_questions.evidence: I think our biggest competitor P&G — like any other big FF [fast-moving] — would be on the leading curve. The challenge would be to bring the people along; you cannot get human out of the loop as fast. The threat would be the new upstarts. If somebody can solve the ESS supplier loop, you can make a company of 10 employees with a billion dollars revenue. ... Two kinds of FMCG: one is the [Mamaearth-shape] of the world — minimal/no manufacturing, everything is contract. There's nothing automated or digitized in between. That is not part of your thesis — manufacturing is not.

routed_to_questions.q_text: What are the parameters used to determine what to outsource versus what to build in-house?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Concrete model: both ways. External engagement gated by PoC; internal build also active. Adoption mode: small pilots, human-refinement, gradual scale, domain-expert tied in.
routed_to_questions.evidence: It's happening both ways. We went to TCS T-HUB. Our leadership team was there. But we are also engaging external AI companies. We are learning. We are eager. ... Startup engagement model: proof of concept required before enterprise adoption. ... Small pilot projects with human refinement, gradual scaling with domain expert involvement.

routed_to_questions.q_text: Walk me through the implementation reality. AI POCs in last 12–18 months — which graduated to production, which died?
routed_to_questions.reasoning: Most P&G internal AI work has not graduated to production — vision stated, build in progress, but 'not yet there'. Implementation barrier is two-layer: infrastructure/change-management + domain-knowledge mapping.
routed_to_questions.evidence: We're trying. We've been trying. ... Not yet there. But that's where the vision is. ... Digitization needs an infrastructure, needs to think differently. The whole information system and the thought process needs to be changed. The challenge would be to bring the people along.

calls.thesis_impact_summary: Added LLM-quality evidence to 19 FMCG questions. Key signals: RGM/trade-promo highest-leverage decision-AI (Q-NODE-FMCG-18-PROBLEMS, conf 0.92); Process Engineering refuted at tier-1 incumbents (Q-NODE-FMCG-08, conf 0.80, refutes); Stackbox vendor confirmed for FMCG supply-chain (Q-NODE-FMCG-04, conf 0.72).
